notes.anchor: I gave Quintus Mucius a letter for you
notes.note: Quintus Mucius is one of the couriers carrying Cicero's letters to Marcellus in exile; the earlier letter he refers to is probably Fam. 4.8. His exact identity is uncertain — he may be the Q. Mucius Scaevola who served as Appius Claudius Pulcher's quaestor in Cilicia, but the bearer here cannot be securely identified.

notes.anchor: your freedman Theophilus is setting out
notes.note: Theophilus, a freedman (libertus) of M. Marcellus, was acting as a messenger between his patron at Mytilene and Cicero at Rome; he carries both this letter and Fam. 4.10. Cicero vouches for his loyalty and goodwill toward Marcellus.

notes.anchor: Everything is referred to one man
notes.note: A frank statement of the new political reality under Caesar's dictatorship: all decisions now rest with one man, who takes counsel from no one — not even his own intimates. Cicero uses this to argue that staying away changes nothing, since power is concentrated regardless.

notes.anchor: when your brother held the consulship from the strength of your authority
notes.note: C. Claudius Marcellus, consul 50 BC (Marcellus's cousin, called "brother"), succeeded M. Marcellus in office; Cicero's point is that Caesar ignored even the counsel of these consuls when they were in power, so he is hardly likely to heed defeated men's opinions now.

notes.anchor: by an honourable retirement you have preserved both the state and the reputation of your own standing
notes.note: Cicero praises Marcellus's honestum otium — his dignified withdrawal from the war — as having safeguarded both his political standing and his good name, but argues that continued absence now risks turning a noble choice into mere obstinacy.

notes.anchor: Great is the licence of swords
notes.note: A warning that armed violence goes unpunished in the lawless conditions of civil war, and even more so in foreign places far from any restraint — a chillingly prescient remark, since Marcellus was in fact murdered at Piraeus in May 45 BC on his way home.
